1: hallĕlû-yāh hallĕlû-ʾēl bĕqodšô hallĕlûhû birqîaʿ ʿuzzô — "Praise the LORD! Praise God in his sanctuary; praise him in the vault of his power!" The Grand Doxology of the Psalter opens with three instances of hallĕlû in one verse. bĕqodšô (in his sanctuary/holiness — either the earthly temple in Jerusalem or the heavenly sanctuary where YHWH dwells; Ps 11:4 places YHWH "in his holy temple" while also "in heaven"). birqîaʿ ʿuzzô (in the firmament/vault of his power — rāqîaʿ from rāqaʿ, to beat out, spread out; the expanse of Gen 1:6–8 stretched out as the created sky; here it is described as ʿuzzô, his power, making the cosmic vault YHWH's display of might). The two locations — sanctuary and firmament — locate praise in both the earthly liturgy and the cosmic order.
2: hallĕlûhû bigĕbûrōtāyw hallĕlûhû kĕrōb gūdlô — "praise him for his mighty deeds; praise him according to the fullness of his greatness." bigĕbûrōtāyw (for/in his mighty deeds — from gĕbûrāh, might, heroic act; plural emphasizes the multiplicity of YHWH's powerful interventions in history: creation, Exodus, conquest, restoration). kĕrōb gūdlô (according to the fullness/abundance of his greatness — rōb from rābab, to be great in number; the overwhelming vastness of YHWH's gōdel, greatness). The praise is to be proportionate to its object: immeasurable greatness warrants immeasurable praise.
3: hallĕlûhû bĕteqaʿ šôpār hallĕlûhû bĕnebel wĕkinnôr — "praise him with the blast of the trumpet; praise him with harp and lyre." šôpār (ram's horn — the oldest and most significant Israelite wind instrument; used to signal the Sinai theophany, Exod 19:13, 16, 19; to assemble the covenant community, Num 10:10; to announce the Jubilee year, Lev 25:9; and to mark warfare and divine arrival, Josh 6). The šôpār begins the instrumental catalog with the instrument most associated with covenant-assembly and divine presence. nebel (harp/lute — a large-bodied stringed instrument, plucked; associated with the prophets, 1 Sam 10:5) + kinnôr (lyre — David's smaller, portable instrument; the instrument of poetry and the Levitical choir).
4: hallĕlûhû bĕtōp ûmāḥôl hallĕlûhû bĕminnîm wĕʿûgāb — "praise him with tambourine and dancing; praise him with strings and flute." tōp (tambourine/hand-drum — the membrane percussion instrument) + māḥôl (dance — the praise-dance; uniquely here dance is listed alongside instruments as a mode of praise). minnîm (strings — from mîn, a kind or class; the plural refers to stringed instruments as a class, not a specific instrument). ʿûgāb (flute/pipe — a wind instrument, possibly a flute or Pan-pipe; the word appears in Gen 4:21 as an instrument of Jubal, "the father of all who play the flute"; rare term, suggesting a specific instrument distinct from the šôpār). The verse uniquely includes dance as a praise-medium alongside instruments, incorporating the full-body worship of Ps 149:3.
5: hallĕlûhû bĕṣilṣĕlê šāmaʿ hallĕlûhû bĕṣilṣĕlê tĕrûʿāh — "praise him with resounding cymbals; praise him with clashing cymbals." Two types of cymbals receive two verses: ṣilṣĕlê šāmaʿ (cymbals of sound/hearing — cymbals that make their sound heard; the šāmaʿ form may indicate a sustained ring) and ṣilṣĕlê tĕrûʿāh (cymbals of the shout/alarm — tĕrûʿāh is the great shout used in warfare, at covenant renewal, and at the sound of the ram's horn, Num 10:5–6; Ps 89:16; Ezra 3:11–13). ṣilṣal is an onomatopoeic word imitating the sound of clashing metal — the word itself rings out. The climactic position of cymbals in the catalog is fitting: they are the loudest percussion, bringing the instrumental praise to its sonic apex before the verbal conclusion of v6.
6: kōl hanĕšāmāh tĕhallēl yāh hallĕlû-yāh — "let everything that has breath praise the LORD! Praise the LORD!" The most expansive possible invitation: kōl hanĕšāmāh (everything that has breath — kōl, all/every; nĕšāmāh, breath, the divine breath of life). nĕšāmāh is the word used in Gen 2:7 for the breath YHWH breathed into Adam: "he breathed into his nostrils the nĕšāmat ḥayyîm (breath of life) and man became a living being." Every creature that carries this original divine gift of breath is included in the final praise. The Psalter that began with "blessed is the man" (Ps 1:1) — a single, righteous individual — ends with kōl hanĕšāmāh — every breathing being without exception. The closing double hallĕlû-yāh seals not just this psalm but the entire Psalter: the final word of the 150-psalm collection is praise.
